summary: My dad, Giovanni Ginocchio, came to the United States in 1864 when he was about 17 or 18 years old. He left Italy to avoid serving in the army, which was mandatory after turning 18. He landed in San Francisco after a six-month journey from Genoa and started working for the Central Pacific Railroad as a laborer and later as a cook. He worked on the railroad for 5 or 6 years, enduring tough conditions in the Sierra Nevada. Later, he returned to Italy, married, and made sure I learned the blacksmith trade before I came to America.

When my dad Giovanni Ginocchio arrived in the United States, he landed in San Francisco after a long six-month voyage from Genoa. He had no relatives or friends there, so he walked up to North Beach and found shelter in an Italian restaurant and hotel. San Francisco was his first stop before he found work with the Central Pacific Railroad. It was a tough start, but it was the gateway for many immigrants like him.

North Beach in San Francisco was where my dad first stayed when he arrived in America. He found an Italian restaurant and hotel there where he made himself at home for about a week until he found a job. It was a place where he could connect with other Italians even though he didn't know anyone personally. This neighborhood was the starting point of his new life in the United States.

My dad worked for the Central Pacific Railroad for about 5 or 6 years, starting shortly after he arrived in America. He was hired in San Francisco and worked on building the railroad from Sacramento to Salt Lake City, Utah. He started as a general laborer helping unload engines and later became a cook, famous for his Italian dishes among the mostly Chinese workforce. He also helped lay track through the Sierra Nevada, enduring wild animals and harsh conditions.

Sacramento was one of the key points where my dad worked while building the Central Pacific Railroad. The railroad construction started from Sacramento heading east toward Salt Lake City. He often talked about the tough work and the wild animals they had to guard against in the Sierra Nevada hills near Sacramento. It was part of the long journey and hard labor that defined his early years in America.

Salt Lake City was the eastern terminus of the Central Pacific Railroad line where my dad worked. The railroad was being built from Sacramento to Salt Lake City during the 1860s, and my dad was part of the crew laying track and supporting the construction. It represented the frontier of the railroad expansion at the time and was a key destination in the transcontinental railroad project.

The Sierra Nevada mountains were a difficult part of the railroad construction where my dad worked for the Central Pacific Railroad. He told me about the nights they had to keep fires burning to keep wild animals like lions away. They often slept outside with snakes crawling near them, which was scary but part of the job. The Sierra Nevada was a tough and wild environment that tested all the workers.

My dad reached what would become Reno, Nevada, while working on the railroad. At that time, Reno was just a crossroads with no official name yet, but my dad sensed it would become a town because of the railroad. The Central Pacific officials were selling lots there for $100 in gold, but my dad couldn't buy any because he only had silver and currency. Many Germans managed to buy lots, and some Italians did too, but my dad had to wait.

Italy was my dad Giovanni Ginocchio's homeland, where he grew up before coming to America in 1864. He left Italy secretly before he turned 18 to avoid being drafted into the army. After working in the U.S. for some years, he returned to Italy to marry and start a family. Italy was the place he escaped from but also where he returned to build his family before coming back to America with us children.

The German people were among the many nationalities working on the Central Pacific Railroad alongside my dad. They were notable because many of them managed to buy lots in what would become Reno, Nevada, using gold. Germans were a significant presence on the railroad and seemed to have some financial advantage compared to others like my dad. They were part of the diverse workforce that built the railroad.

The Chinese people made up the majority of the labor force on the Central Pacific Railroad where my dad worked. They were the main group doing the hard labor of laying track and building the railroad. My dad, who was Italian, became a cook and was appreciated for making Italian dishes, which was a welcome change from the usual Chinese food. The Chinese workers were an essential part of the railroad construction team.

French people were among the many nationalities working on the Central Pacific Railroad with my dad. Alongside Italians, Germans, Chinese, Swedes, Spaniards, and Mexicans, the French contributed to the diverse workforce building the railroad. They all worked together despite language and cultural differences to complete this massive project.

Swedes were part of the diverse group of workers on the Central Pacific Railroad where my dad was employed. They worked alongside Italians, Germans, Chinese, French, Spaniards, and Mexicans. This mix of nationalities was common on the railroad, showing how people from many countries came together for this huge endeavor.

Spaniards were among the various nationalities working on the Central Pacific Railroad with my dad. They were part of the multicultural labor force that included Italians, Germans, Chinese, French, Swedes, and Mexicans. Everyone worked hard to build the railroad despite their different backgrounds.

Mexicans were also part of the diverse group of laborers on the Central Pacific Railroad where my dad worked. Along with Italians, Germans, Chinese, French, Swedes, and Spaniards, they contributed to the construction of the railroad from Sacramento to Salt Lake City. This mix of nationalities was typical of the workforce at that time.

Genoa was the port city in Italy where my dad and his cousin started their journey to America. It took them about six months by ship to reach San Francisco from Genoa. Leaving from Genoa was the beginning of my dad's long and challenging path to a new life in the United States.
